context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly kind of lost on the City Case Study Spider Diagram. I read the assignment, but I still don’t really get what I’m supposed to put in each part or how detailed it should be.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re not lost on content yet—you’re mostly lost on structure. That’s fixable.

At a basic level, the spider diagram asks you to map your case study city against the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation using evidence you find. So for each pathway, you’re looking for what the city is doing, planning, or not doing.

Keep it concise: think short notes, not a full essay.

Start here: what city did you choose, and do you already know the 5 pathways from your course materials, or do you want to list them first from your notes?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief, stay within the assignment, and use guided discovery rather than filling in the diagram for the student. Since the student is confused about structure and level of detail, I should clarify the task at a high level, then ask a focused question that moves directly toward starting their own work. This supports the assignment without giving content they should generate themselves.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, but that’s kind of the problem because now everything about flooding and climate stuff is blurring together for me. I probably saw the 5 pathways before, but I don’t really remember what counts as a pathway versus just like a policy or problem.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now we’ve found the actual blockage: categories.

Think of it this way:
- a problem = what climate risk or challenge the city faces
- a policy/action = something a government or group does
- a pathway = a bigger transformation category that multiple actions can fit under

So don’t start with “everything about Jakarta.” Start smaller.

What are 2–3 climate-related problems you already associate with Jakarta? Just list them roughly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confused about the conceptual frame, so I should narrow the task and prompt recall from course materials rather than supplying the framework or city-specific content directly. I will distinguish categories at a high level—pathway versus policy versus problem—without filling in the assignment. Then I will ask a targeted question that helps the student rebuild the structure themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe flooding, heat, and air pollution? But then I’m not sure if air pollution even belongs here, and then I start second-guessing all of it and feel like I need the whole framework explained before I can list anything.
exchanges.tutor:
Stop trying to make the first list perfect. A rough list is enough to begin.

Flooding and heat are clearly usable. Air pollution may be relevant, but it depends on whether you can connect it to climate action in the course frame.

So take one item only: flooding.
For Jakarta, is flooding a problem, a policy, or a pathway? And what is one concrete thing the city or other actors have done in response?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is hesitating due to uncertainty and wants the framework explained. I should reduce paralysis by validating tentative ideas, clarify that they can separate climate risks from adjacent urban issues, and push them into a manageable first step. I will not provide the full framework or fill the diagram; instead I will ask them to classify one item and identify evidence, moving toward the assignment structure.

exchanges.student: Flooding is the problem, I think. But then for the response I get stuck because there are too many things, like seawalls, relocation, drainage, planning, national government stuff, and I can’t tell what belongs in the diagram versus what’s just extra background.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s not a lack of material. It’s a filtering problem.

For the diagram, “belongs” usually means: a concrete action, plan, or governance approach you can place under a pathway. Background is just context.

Try this test on your list: which 2 items are actual actions or strategies, not just general context? Pick 2 from seawalls, relocation, drainage, planning, national government involvement—and say why each is an action.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed by too many possible responses. I should help them distinguish evidence that belongs in the spider diagram from background by using a selection rule: choose representative actions linked to the five pathways. I will avoid naming the pathways or supplying the city-specific content; instead I’ll ask them to sort items by actor/action and relevance. This keeps it bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I guess seawalls and drainage are actions because they’re actual things being built or managed. But then relocation also seems like an action, and planning does too, so now it just feels like everything counts and I’m back to not knowing how to organize any of it.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—several of them can count. Your mistake is treating this like only one item is allowed.

The real task is organization, not elimination.

Try a rough sort by type:
- physical infrastructure
- governance/planning
- social/community impact

Put seawalls, drainage, relocation, and planning into one of those buckets. Don’t worry about being perfect—just make your best first sort.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling around the same organizing issue. Per the rules, I should point out the redundancy and redirect them to integration. I will give a simple organizing heuristic without solving the content: group by type of action rather than trying to exclude everything. Then I’ll ask for a written sorting attempt, which moves them toward a usable diagram draft.

exchanges.student: Okay, maybe seawalls and drainage = physical infrastructure, planning = governance, relocation = social/community impact? But I still don’t get how those buckets connect to the 5 pathways, and I’m worried I’m making up my own system instead of doing the assignment right.
exchanges.tutor:
Those buckets are not your final answer—they’re just scaffolding to stop the confusion. That part is fine.

Now go back to your course page/notes and pull the exact names of the 5 pathways. Use the course’s wording, not yours.

Once you have them, try matching just one item—say seawalls or planning—to one pathway. Which pathway name seems closest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced a useful preliminary sort but is anxious about alignment with the actual assignment framework. I should validate the sort as a draft, clarify that the buckets are only a temporary thinking tool, and redirect them back to the course materials for the authoritative pathway names. To stay Socratic and avoid giving the framework directly, I will ask them to retrieve the 5 pathway labels and then match one item to one pathway. This moves the student toward the actual spider diagram without completing it for them.

exchanges.student: I’m looking at my notes and I still feel stuck because the pathway names sound broad enough that seawalls or planning could probably fit in more than one. I don’t really trust my match at all.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s normal. Broad categories often overlap.

Your job is not to find the only possible match. Your job is to make a defensible best-fit choice.

Use this rule: place the item under the pathway that captures its main purpose, then justify it in a short phrase.

Pick one now: for seawalls, what is the main purpose—physical protection, institutional coordination, behavior/community change, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again stuck on ambiguity. I should normalize that overlap exists, give a decision rule for choosing the best-fit pathway, and ask for a justification rather than a perfect match. This preserves guided discovery and avoids providing the actual mapping. The response should remain brief and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: I think physical protection, but I’m still not sure if that means I’m supposed to ignore the governance side completely, and then I start feeling like I need a full example of how one row or branch is supposed to look.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t ignore governance; just don’t let it control the main placement.

Think of one branch/row as:
pathway name → one Jakarta action → a few words on what it does/why it fits

Main fit first, secondary aspects optional in a brief note.

Now try drafting that structure yourself for seawalls in plain notes—not polished writing. What would your 3 parts be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for an example format due to uncertainty. I should avoid supplying a completed row, but I can describe the structure of a row/branch in abstract terms and reinforce that secondary aspects can be noted briefly without changing the main placement. This answers the formatting confusion while keeping the student responsible for generating content.

exchanges.student: I’d write something like: [pathway name?] → seawalls/coastal defense → protects against flooding and sea level rise. But that still feels so generic that I can’t tell if I’m doing the assignment or just writing obvious facts.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right shape. The weakness is not the structure—it’s the specificity.

Make it less generic by adding just one anchor:
- who is doing it
- where in Jakarta
- what plan/program it connects to

Pick one of those three. Which one can you identify for seawalls from your source?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a workable structure but doubts the level of specificity. I should reassure them that concise notes are appropriate for the spider diagram, then push them to add one layer of specificity using actor, location, or plan/program detail. I must not supply the actual content or wording, only a criterion and a question that moves them forward.

exchanges.student: I can probably find what plan it connects to, but I’m confused about how much source detail is too much for a spider diagram. If I start adding plan names and actors and locations, I feel like it turns into a research summary instead of the diagram.
exchanges.tutor:
Good concern. For a spider diagram, aim for note-level evidence, not paragraph-level summary.

Rule of thumb: each branch/row should have just enough detail to prove it’s a real Jakarta action, not a vague idea. Usually 1 specific anchor is enough.

So for your seawalls branch, what single anchor will you use: actor, plan name, or location?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about level of detail, so I should set a practical boundary without doing the work for them. I’ll give a concise rule of thumb for note-level detail, distinguish diagram notes from background summary, and then ask them to apply that rule to one branch. This keeps the response bite-sized, assignment-focused, and Socratic.
